texts.full_text: stories, and dreams. I had been so immersed, first in writing it, and then in the euphoria of having it published, that I didn't think about the kids. Then I got my first fan letter from a little girl in Chicago. "I liked your book because it made me happy," she wrote. "Thank you for writing this book." (What a review! Children not only make great readers, they are the best reviewers.) A friend recently sent the photo above of her God-son reading my new Halloween book, LITTLE GOBLINS TEN. I love seeing it in his hands, where it belongs. And if he wants to review it, that's fine by me too. texts.full_text: mply a monotonicity constraint in the Hubble diagram, which we confront to present-day supernova data. We use the running gradient method of statistical inference by Hall & Heckman (2000). We find no significant departure from monotonicity. The method seems well adapted and we recommend its use with future data.' author: - | N. Meinshausen, J. Rice and T. Schücker\ *University of California, Berkeley, and Université de Provence, Marseille*\ nicolai@stat.berkeley.edu\ rice@stat.berkeley.edu\ schucker@cpt.univ-mrs.fr title: Testing for monotonicity in the Hubble diagram --- Introduction ============ The Hubble diagram is a fundamental tool in cosmology, relating the redshift of celestial objects to their distance from us. The observation that this relationship appears linear at low redshifts led to a deeper understanding of the expanding universe and has been crucial for estimating key parameters such as the Hubble constant (H0). However, on larger scales or higher redshifts, deviations from linearity can occur due to various factors including the acceleration of the expansion itself. General relativistic kinematics combined with the cosmological principle provide a framework within which we expect certain monotonic behaviors in this diagram; specifically, that as one looks further out into space (and thus back in time), objects should appear more redshifted and faint
